subject: AWS
description: Amazon Web Services (AWS) is a comprehensive cloud computing platform offering infrastructure-as-a-service, platform-as-a-service, and software-as-a-service solutions.

questions.question: What is the difference between an AWS Region and an Availability Zone (AZ), and how do they work together?
questions.answer:
AWS Region: A separate geographical area (like N. Virginia or Ireland), isolated from others for reliability.

Availability Zone (AZ): A distinct data center within a Region, with independent power and networking.

AZs are connected via low-latency, high-throughput networks. Deploying resources across multiple AZs ensures high availability and fault tolerance in case one AZ fails.
wrongAnswers: Regions are inside Availability Zones
AZs span multiple geographic areas
They are the same thing with different names

questions.question: Explain the AWS Shared Responsibility Model.
questions.answer:
AWS secures the infrastructure—hardware, software, and data centers.

Customers manage data, access, and application security.

By service type:
- IaaS: Customers manage OS and data
- PaaS: AWS manages the platform; customers handle data
- SaaS/Serverless: Customers focus on code and permissions
wrongAnswers: AWS manages all security including customer data
Customers manage the physical infrastructure
The model is the same for all AWS services

questions.question: What is an EC2 instance, and what are the factors you consider when choosing an instance type?
questions.answer:
EC2 provides scalable computing power with complete control over the OS.

Choose based on workload needs:
- Burstable (T-family): Low/variable traffic; earns CPU credits
- Fixed-performance: Steady, high-demand workloads

Pricing:
- On-Demand: Maximum flexibility, pay-as-you-go
- Reserved Instances/Savings Plans: Significant discounts
- Spot Instances: Cheapest for interruptible jobs
wrongAnswers: Instance type only affects the price, not performance
All instance types have the same CPU and memory
EC2 instances cannot be scaled after creation

questions.question: What is Amazon S3, and what guarantees does it provide for durability and availability?
questions.answer:
Amazon S3 is scalable object storage for backups, archives, data lakes, and static hosting.

Guarantees:
- 11 nines (99.999999999%) durability by replicating across multiple AZs
- ~99.99% availability (S3 Standard)

Durability protects against data loss; availability ensures access when needed.
wrongAnswers: S3 provides block storage like EBS
S3 data is stored in a single data center only
Durability and availability mean the same thing

questions.question: Explain the purpose of a Virtual Private Cloud (VPC). What are its core components?
questions.answer:
VPC creates a secure, isolated network within AWS.

Core Components:
- Subnets: Public & private networks
- Route tables: Define traffic paths
- IGW/NAT: Internet for public (IGW) and outbound-only for private (NAT)
- Security Groups: Stateful, instance-level firewall
- NACLs: Stateless, subnet-level firewall
wrongAnswers: VPC is a physical data center owned by AWS
VPC only provides compute resources
Security Groups and NACLs are the same thing

questions.question: What is IAM, and why is it critical for security? Differentiate between an IAM User, Group, and Role.
questions.answer:
IAM manages access to AWS resources securely, enforcing least privilege.

- IAM Users: Individuals/apps with long-term credentials
- IAM Groups: Collections of users to apply shared policies
- IAM Roles: Temporary, auto-rotated credentials assumed by trusted entities (EC2, Lambda)
wrongAnswers: IAM Users and IAM Roles are identical
Groups can directly launch EC2 instances
IAM is only needed for S3 access

questions.question: What is the difference between stopping and terminating an EC2 instance?
questions.answer:
Stopping:
- Shuts down but preserves state and data
- Root EBS volume remains attached
- Can be restarted later
- No compute charges, but EBS storage costs continue

Terminating:
- Permanently deletes the instance
- Root EBS volume deleted by default
- Instance cannot be restarted
- All charges stop
wrongAnswers: Stopping permanently deletes the instance
Terminating only pauses the instance temporarily
Both result in permanent data loss

questions.question: What is an Amazon Machine Image (AMI)?
questions.answer:
AMI is a pre-configured template for launching EC2 instances.

Components:
- Root volume template: OS, app server, software
- Launch permissions: Private, shared, or public
- Block device mapping: Attached storage definitions
wrongAnswers: AMI is a type of EC2 instance
AMIs can only be created by AWS
AMI is used for S3 bucket configuration

questions.question: What is the purpose of Amazon CloudWatch?
questions.answer:
CloudWatch is a monitoring and observability service.

Key capabilities:
- Metrics: Auto-collected (EC2, S3, RDS) + custom metrics
- Logs: Centralized ingest/storage/search
- Alarms: Threshold-based alerts on metrics
- Events/Rules: Near real-time resource change events
wrongAnswers: CloudWatch is a database service
CloudWatch can only monitor S3 buckets
CloudWatch is used for sending emails only

questions.question: Differentiate between horizontal and vertical scaling.
questions.answer:
Vertical (scale up):
- Move to a bigger instance (CPU/RAM/I/O)
- Simple but has hard limits
- May need downtime
- Single point of failure

Horizontal (scale out):
- Add more instances behind a load balancer
- Cloud-native, near-limitless scale
- Higher availability/fault tolerance
wrongAnswers: Horizontal scaling adds more hardware resources to the same machine
Vertical scaling is always better than horizontal scaling
Both types require the same implementation effort

questions.question: Compare and contrast Security Groups and Network ACLs (NACLs).
questions.answer:
Security Groups (stateful):
- Allow inbound → matching return traffic auto-allowed
- Fewer rules to manage

NACLs (stateless):
- Return traffic must be explicitly allowed
- Permit outbound ephemeral ports 1024-65535 for responses
wrongAnswers: Security Groups are stateless while NACLs are stateful
Both are stateful firewalls at different levels
NACLs are evaluated before Security Groups

questions.question: How would you provide an EC2 instance in a private subnet with internet access for software patches?
questions.answer:
Use a NAT Gateway:
1. Deploy NAT Gateway in a public subnet with Elastic IP
2. Route internet-bound traffic from private subnet to NAT Gateway
3. Update Route Table: Add route (0.0.0.0/0) pointing to NAT Gateway

Alternative: NAT Instance (less scalable, requires manual management)
wrongAnswers: Attach an Elastic IP directly to the private instance
Use a Security Group to allow outbound traffic
Deploy the instance in a public subnet instead

questions.question: Explain the differences between S3, EBS, and EFS storage services.
questions.answer:
S3: Object storage accessed via HTTP/S
- Ideal for backups, static assets, data lakes
- Not mountable as a file system

EBS: Block storage attached to ONE EC2 instance
- Low-latency, high IOPS
- Best for boot volumes and databases

EFS: File storage mountable across MULTIPLE EC2s via NFS
- Great for shared environments, CMS, code repos
- Scales automatically
wrongAnswers: S3, EBS, and EFS are all block storage services
EBS can be attached to multiple EC2 instances
EFS is cheaper than EBS for all use cases

questions.question: When would you choose RDS versus DynamoDB?
questions.answer:
Choose RDS when:
- Structured, relational data with well-defined schemas
- Complex queries, multi-table joins, ACID compliance
- Financial systems, e-commerce, CRMs

Choose DynamoDB when:
- High scalability and low-latency at massive scale
- Semi-structured or schema-flexible data
- User profiles, IoT telemetry, session data
- Key-value or simple document operations
wrongAnswers: RDS is always cheaper than DynamoDB
DynamoDB does not support any queries
Use RDS for unstructured data storage

questions.question: What is an Elastic Load Balancer (ELB)? Describe the different types.
questions.answer:
ELB distributes incoming traffic across multiple targets, improving availability and fault tolerance.

Types:
- Application LB (ALB): Layer 7, HTTP/HTTPS, content-based routing, microservices
- Network LB (NLB): Layer 4, millions of req/sec, ultra-low latency, static IPs
- Gateway LB (GLB): Layer 3, third-party appliances, deep packet inspection
- Classic LB (CLB): Legacy, both Layer 4/7, deprecated
wrongAnswers: ELB only works with EC2 instances
All ELB types operate at Layer 7 only
ELB cannot distribute traffic across multiple AZs

questions.question: Explain Auto Scaling Group (ASG). What components are needed to configure one?
questions.answer:
ASG ensures right number of EC2 instances to handle demand.

Key components:
- Launch Template/Config: EC2 blueprint (AMI, type, SGs, user data)
- ASG: min/max/desired capacity + VPC/subnets
- Target tracking: Keeps metric (e.g., CPU %) near target
- Step/Simple scaling: Scales on CloudWatch alarm thresholds
- Scheduled scaling: Scales at set times
wrongAnswers: ASG only scales up, never scales down
ASG requires manual intervention to add instances
ASG cannot span multiple Availability Zones

questions.question: What is Infrastructure as Code (IaC), and what is the role of AWS CloudFormation?
questions.answer:
IaC manages infrastructure through code instead of manual processes.

CloudFormation models AWS/third-party resources:
- Automation & consistency: Provision full environments from templates
- Version control: Store in Git for diffs, reviews, rollbacks
- Repeatability: Recreate identical stacks in other regions
wrongAnswers: CloudFormation is a container orchestration service
IaC cannot be version controlled
CloudFormation only manages EC2 instances

questions.question: Explain different S3 Storage Classes and S3 Lifecycle Policies.
questions.answer:
Storage Classes:
- S3 Standard: Frequently accessed, high performance
- S3 Intelligent-Tiering: Auto-shifts based on usage
- S3 Standard-IA & One Zone-IA: Infrequently accessed
- S3 Glacier: Archival with Instant/Flexible/Deep Archive retrieval

Lifecycle Policies automate:
- Transitioning objects to cheaper storage
- Expiring objects after set period
wrongAnswers: All S3 storage classes have the same price
S3 Glacier requires manual data retrieval only
Lifecycle policies cannot delete objects automatically

questions.question: What is the difference between Elastic IP and Public IP address?
questions.answer:
Public IP:
- Auto-assigned in public subnets
- Changes on stop/start
- Released on termination
- Not suitable for stable endpoints

Elastic IP (EIP):
- Static IPv4 owned by the account
- Attach/detach to instances/ENIs
- Supports quick failover and stable DNS endpoints
wrongAnswers: Elastic IP and Public IP are the same thing
Public IPs persist when instances are stopped
Elastic IPs are free to use

questions.question: What is the purpose of AWS Lambda? Compare it to EC2.
questions.answer:
Lambda: Serverless, event-driven compute
- No servers to manage
- Auto handles scaling, patching, capacity
- Triggers: API Gateway, S3, SQS, etc.

vs EC2:
- Management: Lambda is fully managed; EC2 gives OS control
- State: Lambda is stateless & short-lived (max 15 min); EC2 persists
- Scaling: Lambda auto-scales; EC2 needs ASG
- Pricing: Lambda = requests + execution time; EC2 = uptime
wrongAnswers: Lambda requires managing servers like EC2
Lambda functions can run indefinitely like EC2
Lambda is always more expensive than EC2

questions.question: Design a serverless API backend. Which AWS services would you use?
questions.answer:
Serverless API Architecture:
- API Gateway: Entry point for REST endpoints, routing, throttling, auth
- Auth: Cognito for user auth/JWTs; Lambda Authorizers for custom tokens
- Lambda: Stateless business logic with auto-scaling
- DynamoDB: Low-latency NoSQL for persistent data
- CloudWatch: Logs/metrics; SAM or CloudFormation for IaC
wrongAnswers: EC2 instances are required for serverless APIs
S3 can replace API Gateway for API hosting
Serverless APIs require manual server management

questions.question: Compare ECS and EKS. When would you choose one over the other?
questions.answer:
ECS (Elastic Container Service):
- AWS-native, tight IAM/VPC/ELB integration
- Best for apps not needing multi-cloud portability
- Use when speed and minimal overhead are priorities

EKS (Elastic Kubernetes Service):
- Managed Kubernetes, full K8s ecosystem (Helm, Prometheus)
- Requires deeper K8s expertise
- Use when team is committed to Kubernetes
wrongAnswers: ECS and EKS are the same service
EKS does not support Kubernetes workloads
ECS requires less expertise than EKS

questions.question: How would you design a highly available architecture for a critical web application?
questions.answer:
Multi-AZ Architecture:
- ALB: Multi-AZ, health checks, reroutes from unhealthy targets
- Web/App Tier: EC2 ASG across ≥2 AZs, auto-replace, scale with demand
- Database: RDS Multi-AZ for synchronous standby and automatic failover

Result: Eliminates single points of failure, maintains availability under all conditions.
wrongAnswers: Single AZ deployment is highly available
HA requires only a load balancer
Database does not need to be highly available

questions.question: Explain the difference between VPC Peering and AWS Transit Gateway.
questions.answer:
VPC Peering:
- One-to-one connection between two VPCs
- Non-transitive routing
- Doesn't scale well: n VPCs need n(n-1)/2 links
- Best for small-scale deployments

Transit Gateway:
- Hub-and-spoke model with central gateway
- Transitive routing (VPCs communicate without direct peering)
- Each VPC needs only one attachment
- Ideal for large multi-VPC or hybrid cloud setups
wrongAnswers: VPC Peering supports transitive routing
Transit Gateway requires a direct connection for each VPC pair
They are functionally identical

questions.question: How does AWS KMS work, and what is envelope encryption?
questions.answer:
KMS: Managed service for cryptographic keys with FIPS 140-2 validated HSMs.

Envelope Encryption:
1. Generate Data Key: App calls KMS → gets plaintext DEK + ciphertext DEK
2. Encrypt data: Use plaintext DEK in memory to encrypt
3. Store securely: Discard plaintext DEK; store ciphertext data + ciphertext DEK together
wrongAnswers: KMS encrypts data directly without using data keys
Envelope encryption only works with S3
KMS stores the actual data to encrypt

questions.question: What is a cold start in AWS Lambda, and how do you mitigate it?
questions.answer:
Cold start: Delay when no pre-initialized execution environment is available.

AWS must:
- Allocate new container
- Download function code and dependencies
- Initialize runtime
- Execute initialization code

Mitigation strategies:
- Provisioned Concurrency
- Optimize deployment package size
- Choose efficient runtimes
- Optimize initialization code
- VPC Configuration improvements
wrongAnswers: Cold starts only happen on first Lambda invocation
Cold starts can be completely eliminated with default settings
Provisioned Concurrency increases cold start frequency

questions.question: What is the purpose of S3 Object Lock and MFA Delete?
questions.answer:
S3 Object Lock:
- Write-Once-Read-Many (WORM) protection
- Prevents deletion or modification of objects
- For compliance and data immutability

MFA Delete:
- Extra layer of protection against accidental/malicious deletions
- Requires MFA authentication to delete
wrongAnswers: S3 Object Lock prevents reading objects
MFA Delete protects against unauthorized uploads
They are the same feature with different names

questions.question: How would you design a CI/CD pipeline for a containerized application on AWS?
questions.answer:
CI/CD Pipeline Components:
1. Source Control: CodeCommit/GitHub → triggers pipeline
2. Orchestration: CodePipeline (Source → Build → Deploy)
3. Build/Test: CodeBuild with buildspec.yml, runs tests, security scans
4. Container Registry: ECR stores and versions Docker images
5. Deployment: CodeDeploy for ECS or kubectl/Helm for EKS
wrongAnswers: CI/CD is only for serverless applications
EC2 is required for running CI/CD pipelines
Containers cannot be deployed through CI/CD

questions.question: What are EC2 Placement Groups, and when would you use them?
questions.answer:
Cluster: Tight packing for low latency/bandwidth (HPC, big data)

Partition: Isolated partitions across racks/AZs (Hadoop, Cassandra, Kafka)

Spread: Each instance on distinct physical rack (critical workloads like primary/standby DBs)

Use for:
- Performance-sensitive apps needing fast inter-instance comms
- Fault-tolerant architectures isolating failure domains
- Compliance requiring physical separation
wrongAnswers: Placement Groups are required for all EC2 instances
Spread placement guarantees instances are in the same rack
Cluster placement provides maximum fault tolerance

questions.question: Explain the AWS Well-Architected Framework and its pillars.
questions.answer:
AWS Well-Architected Framework pillars:

1. Operational Excellence: Run/monitor systems, improve processes
2. Security: Protect data, systems, assets through risk management
3. Reliability: Recover from failures, maintain availability
4. Performance Efficiency: Optimize resources, adapt to changes
5. Cost Optimization: Minimize expenses, maximize value
wrongAnswers: The framework only covers security pillars
Well-Architected is optional and has no best practices
Cost Optimization is the only important pillar

questions.question: A web app behind ALB is experiencing intermittent 502 errors. How would you diagnose and resolve this?
questions.answer:
Diagnosis approach:
1. ALB telemetry: Check CloudWatch (HTTPCode_Target_5XX_Count, HealthyHostCount), parse ALB access logs
2. EC2/app health: Review CPU/network/disk; inspect app logs for timeouts, OOM, DB connection limits
3. Network/security: Verify SGs/NACLs permit ALB ↔ EC2 traffic including ephemeral ports

Resolution based on root cause findings.
wrongAnswers: 502 errors indicate the ALB is overloaded, restart it
Increase ALB size to fix 502 errors
502 errors are always caused by client-side issues

questions.question: Your AWS bill unexpectedly doubled. How would you identify the root cause and recommend cost-saving measures?
questions.answer:
Step-by-step process:
1. Billing Dashboard: Compare current vs previous months
2. Cost Explorer: Analyze by service, region, usage type, tags
3. Service-specific tools:
   - EC2: Trusted Advisor/Compute Optimizer for idle instances
   - S3: Storage Lens for access patterns, lifecycle issues
   - Data Transfer: Check outbound/inter-AZ traffic
4. CloudTrail: Audit API activity for suspicious calls

Cost-saving: Terminate unused resources, right-size instances, use Savings Plans, Spot for fault-tolerant workloads
wrongAnswers: AWS billing errors cannot be investigated
Delete all resources to reduce costs
Cost savings only apply to S3 storage

questions.question: Design a scalable architecture for a global photo-sharing application.
questions.answer:
Global Photo App Architecture:
- Ingestion: S3 (Transfer Acceleration) stores uploads; Lambda processes/thumbnails
- Delivery: CloudFront caches globally; origin = S3
- API/Auth: API Gateway + Lambda for REST; Cognito for auth
- Data: DynamoDB (Global Tables) for metadata; OpenSearch for feeds
- DNS: Route 53 latency-based routing to nearest region
- Monitoring: CloudWatch; IAM least-privilege; S3 Lifecycle
wrongAnswers: Use a single EC2 instance for the entire application
Global applications require manual region management
S3 alone can serve photos globally without CDN

questions.question: IAM access keys were committed to a public GitHub repository. What are your immediate and long-term steps?
questions.answer:
Immediate:
- Revoke exposed keys and active role sessions
- Contain the blast radius

Investigate:
- CloudTrail traces API activity, privilege changes, unauthorized resource creation

Remediate:
- Rotate related creds (DB/API/SSH)
- Enable Secrets Manager rotation
- Kill unauthorized resources
- Scan for backdoors

Prevent:
- Add secret scanning to CI/CD
- Train developers
- Run post-mortem, update incident response plan
wrongAnswers: Delete the GitHub commit history to remove the keys
Rotate keys only after 90 days
Keys in public repos are automatically encrypted by AWS

questions.question: Migrate a 10TB on-premises Oracle database to AWS with minimal downtime.
questions.answer:
Migration Strategy (using DMS):
1. Assess & Prepare: Analyze source; use AWS SCT for schema conversion; provision target RDS (Multi-AZ)
2. Full Load: Create DMS replication instance/endpoints; run full load with source online
3. CDC: Enable near-real-time change capture to keep target in sync
4. Validate & Cutover: During short window, stop app → final changes → verify data → switch to RDS endpoint

Result: Minimizes downtime to only final cutover window.
wrongAnswers: Take the database offline for the entire migration
DMS requires rewriting all SQL queries
Migration must be done manually file by file

questions.question: Design a scalable network for a large enterprise with dozens of VPCs across multiple AWS accounts.
questions.answer:
Hub-and-Spoke with Transit Gateway:

1. Transit Gateway (Network account): Regional cloud router, enables centralized routing

2. VPC Attachments: Each VPC attaches to TGW, eliminating VPC peering meshes

3. Direct Connect: Dedicated connection to on-prem via Direct Connect Gateway → TGW

4. Multi-Account: Use RAM to share TGW; SCPs and tagging for governance

Benefits: Centralized control, simplified routing, scalable connectivity.
wrongAnswers: Create individual VPC peering connections between all VPCs
Use a single VPC for the entire enterprise
Transit Gateway cannot connect multiple AWS accounts

questions.question: A Lambda function integrated with API Gateway is intermittently timing out in a VPC accessing RDS.
questions.answer:
Troubleshooting steps:
1. Analyze Logs/Traces: CloudWatch Logs for timeout errors; X-Ray for latency hotspots
2. Investigate Causes:
   - VPC Cold Starts: ENI creation adds latency → enable Provisioned Concurrency
   - DB Connections: Exhaust RDS limits → use RDS Proxy
   - RDS Performance: Monitor via CloudWatch/Performance Insights
   - VPC Networking: Verify subnet routing, SGs, NACLs
3. Remediate incrementally; load test to confirm resolution.
wrongAnswers: Increase Lambda timeout to 15 minutes
Remove the VPC configuration from Lambda
Restart API Gateway to fix timeouts

questions.question: Design a system to ingest and process real-time IoT data from thousands of sensors.
questions.answer:
Real-time IoT Architecture:
1. Ingestion: Kinesis Data Streams for high-velocity data with durability and ordering
2. Processing:
   - Lambda: Lightweight filtering, enrichment, alerting
   - Kinesis Data Analytics: Continuous SQL queries for aggregations, anomaly detection
3. Storage: S3 for raw/processed data lake
4. Analytics: Athena for ad-hoc SQL queries; Redshift for BI
5. Security: IAM roles, KMS encryption, VPC endpoints; CloudWatch monitoring
wrongAnswers: Use SQS for real-time IoT data ingestion
EC2 instances are required for IoT processing
IoT data cannot be processed in real-time on AWS

questions.question: How would you design a zero-trust networking environment for microservices on EKS?
questions.answer:
Zero-Trust Architecture for EKS:

- Strong Identity (IRSA): Each microservice gets its own least-privilege IAM role; avoid node-level roles

- Micro-segmentation: Default deny-all NetworkPolicy; add narrow allows

- Encrypted Comms: Service mesh (Istio/Linkerd/App Mesh) with mTLS and identity-based access

- Ingress/Egress Control: ALB Ingress + TLS + WAF; outbound via NAT/VPC Endpoints with strict policies
wrongAnswers: Zero-trust allows all traffic by default
Use Security Groups at the node level only
Network Policies are optional in zero-trust

questions.question: Design a disaster recovery strategy with RTO of 15 minutes and RPO of 1 minute.
questions.answer:
Recommended: Warm Standby Strategy

Setup:
- Secondary DR region maintains scaled-down but fully functional environment
- Multi-AZ networking, minimal ASG, load balancer

Data Replication:
- Aurora Global Database or DynamoDB Global Tables for near real-time sync
- S3 Cross-Region Replication (CRR)

Failover:
- Automated via Route 53 health checks
- DNS routes to DR region; ASG scales to full capacity
- Replicated DB promoted to primary

Result: Meets 15-min RTO and 1-min RPO requirements.
wrongAnswers: Backup to S3 daily is sufficient for 1-min RPO
DR region is not needed for disaster recovery
Manual failover can meet 15-min RTO requirement

questions.question: What is AWS Lambda and what are its key features?
questions.answer:
AWS Lambda is a serverless compute service that runs code without servers.

Key Features:
- Event-driven: Triggers from 200+ AWS services
- Auto-scaling: Handles from 0 to thousands of requests
- Pay-per-use: No charge for idle time
- Multiple runtimes: Node.js, Python, Java, Go, Ruby, .NET
- Integration: Deep integration with AWS services
- Stateless: State stored externally (DynamoDB, S3)
wrongAnswers: Lambda requires provisioning and managing servers
Lambda functions run continuously 24/7
Lambda only supports JavaScript

questions.question: Explain Amazon RDS and its supported database engines.
questions.answer:
Amazon RDS (Relational Database Service) is a managed DB service.

Supported Engines:
- Amazon Aurora (MySQL/PostgreSQL compatible)
- PostgreSQL
- MySQL
- MariaDB
- Oracle
- SQL Server

Key Features:
- Automated backups and patching
- Multi-AZ deployment
- Read replicas for scaling
- Encryption at rest and in transit
wrongAnswers: RDS only supports Oracle database
RDS requires manual backup management
RDS does not support read replicas

questions.question: What is Amazon DynamoDB and when should you use it?
questions.answer:
DynamoDB is a fully managed NoSQL database.

When to Use:
- Serverless applications
- High-scale workloads (millions of requests/sec)
- Low-latency requirements
- Key-value or document data models
- Pay-per-request pricing

Features:
- Global Tables for multi-region replication
- DAX (DynamoDB Accelerator) for in-memory caching
- On-demand and provisioned capacity modes
wrongAnswers: DynamoDB requires managing your own servers
DynamoDB only supports key-value operations
DynamoDB cannot scale beyond 1000 requests per second

questions.question: What are the different types of AWS storage services?
questions.answer:
AWS Storage Services:

Object Storage:
- S3: 11 nines durability, HTTP accessible

Block Storage:
- EBS: Persistent block storage for EC2

File Storage:
- EFS: Shared file system for EC2 (NFS)
- FSx: Windows file system or Lustre

Archive:
- S3 Glacier: Low-cost archival storage

Backup:
- AWS Backup: Centralized backup service
wrongAnswers: S3 is block storage like EBS
EFS can only attach to one EC2 instance
AWS has only one storage service

questions.question: What is Amazon CloudFront and how does it work?
questions.answer:
CloudFront is a Content Delivery Network (CDN).

How it works:
- Caches content at edge locations worldwide
- Reduces latency by serving from nearest location
- Integrates with WAF for security
- Supports dynamic and static content

Key Features:
- 450+ edge locations
- DDoS protection (Shield Standard)
- Lambda@Edge for customization
- Field-level encryption
wrongAnswers: CloudFront stores data permanently at edge locations
CloudFront can only cache static content
CloudFront is only available in US regions

questions.question: What is AWS Route 53 and what routing policies does it support?
questions.answer:
Route 53 is a scalable DNS web service.

Routing Policies:
- Simple: Single resource
- Weighted: Traffic split by percentage
- Latency-based: Lowest latency route
- Failover: Primary to secondary on failure
- Geolocation: Based on user location
- Geoproximity: Based on geography + bias
- IP-based: Based on user IP
- Multi-value: Multiple healthy resources
wrongAnswers: Route 53 only supports simple routing
All routing policies work the same way
Route 53 cannot route traffic based on latency

questions.question: Explain Amazon SNS and SQS. When would you use each?
questions.answer:
SQS (Simple Queue Service):
- Message queue: Decouples producers/consumers
- Types: Standard (at-least-once) and FIFO (exactly-once)
- Polling model: Consumers pull messages
- No subscriber management

SNS (Simple Notification Service):
- Pub/Sub: One message to many subscribers
- Push model: Pushes to subscribers (Lambda, SQS, HTTP, email, SMS)
- Topics for fan-out patterns
wrongAnswers: SNS and SQS are the same service
SQS pushes messages to subscribers like SNS
SNS stores messages until consumed like SQS

questions.question: What is AWS Direct Connect and what are its benefits?
questions.answer:
Direct Connect provides dedicated network connection from on-premises to AWS.

Benefits:
- Consistent network performance
- Reduced bandwidth costs
- Private connectivity (no internet)
- Higher throughput (1Gbps to 100Gbps)
- Compatible with VPC, public services, and S3

Use cases:
- Large dataset transfers
- Hybrid cloud architectures
- Consistent latency requirements
wrongAnswers: Direct Connect uses the public internet
Direct Connect is only for S3 access
Direct Connect provides lower throughput than VPN

questions.question: What is AWS WAF and how does it protect web applications?
questions.answer:
AWS WAF is a web application firewall.

Protection Features:
- SQL injection and XSS protection
- IP blocking/allowing
- Rate limiting
- Geo-blocking
- Bot control

Integration:
- CloudFront
- Application Load Balancer
- API Gateway
- AppSync

Rules:
- Managed rules (AWS, vendors)
- Custom rules (IP sets, regex)
wrongAnswers: WAF only protects against DDoS attacks
WAF cannot block IP addresses
WAF works without any integration

questions.question: Explain AWS CloudTrail and its use cases.
questions.answer:
CloudTrail records API calls across AWS account.

Use Cases:
- Security analysis: Detect unauthorized access
- Compliance: Audit trail for regulations
- Operational troubleshooting: Who did what, when
- Cost optimization: Track resource changes

Features:
- Management events and data events
- Log file validation (integrity)
- CloudWatch Logs integration
- S3 log storage

questions.question: What is cloud computing?
questions.answer:

questions.question: What are the three types of cloud deployment models?
questions.answer:
Three deployment models:

1. Private Cloud: Used by a single organization, not exposed to public. Ideal for sensitive applications requiring strict control.

2. Public Cloud: Resources owned and operated by third-party providers like AWS, Azure. Available to anyone over the internet.

3. Hybrid Cloud: Combination of private and public clouds. Keeps some servers on-premises while extending capabilities to the cloud.

questions.question: What are the five characteristics of cloud computing?
questions.answer:
Five characteristics of cloud computing:

1. On-demand self-service: Users can provision services without human interaction

2. Broad network access: Services available over network via standard mechanisms

3. Multi-tenancy and resource pooling: Resources dynamically assigned based on demand

4. Rapid elasticity and scalability: Auto-scale up/down quickly

5. Measured service: Usage monitored, controlled, and billed transparently
wrongAnswers: Measured service means customers pay flat monthly fees

questions.question: What are the three main types of cloud computing services (IaaS, PaaS, SaaS)?

1. IaaS (Infrastructure as a Service): Provides basic building blocks like compute, storage, networking. Examples: EC2, S3, VPC

2. PaaS (Platform as a Service): Managed platform for developing, deploying, managing apps without building infrastructure. Examples: Elastic Beanstalk, Heroku

3. SaaS (Software as a Service): Complete end-user applications running in cloud. Examples: SES, Google Docs, Salesforce CRM
wrongAnswers: SaaS provides raw infrastructure components
PaaS requires managing the underlying operating system

questions.question: What are the key security best practices for AWS EC2?
questions.answer:
Essential EC2 security practices:

1. Use IAM for access management with least privilege
2. Restrict access to trusted hosts only
3. Minimize permissions - only grant what's needed
4. Disable password-based logins for AMIs
5. Implement Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA)
6. Use Security Groups as firewalls
7. Regularly patch and update instances
8. Enable VPC for network isolation
wrongAnswers: EC2 security groups allow all inbound traffic by default

questions.question: How do you ensure multi-region redundancy in AWS architecture?
questions.answer:
Multi-region redundancy strategies:

1. Deploy critical resources (EC2, RDS, S3) in multiple AWS Regions
2. Use Route 53 for geo-based DNS routing and failover
3. Implement S3 Cross-Region Replication for data backup
4. Configure active-active or active-passive failover
5. Use CloudWatch for monitoring performance
6. Leverage AWS Global Accelerator for improved latency
7. Test failover procedures regularly
wrongAnswers: Multi-region redundancy requires manual data synchronization
Route 53 cannot route traffic between regions
S3 replication does not support cross-region copying

questions.question: What strategies create a highly available and fault-tolerant AWS architecture?
questions.answer:
High availability strategies:

1. Implement redundancy across all components
2. Use load balancing to distribute traffic evenly
3. Set up automated monitoring for real-time failure detection
4. Design for scalability to handle varying loads
5. Implement fault isolation and regular backups
6. Create comprehensive disaster recovery plans
7. Design for graceful degradation
8. Practice continuous testing and deployment
wrongAnswers: High availability means using a single instance in one AZ
Load balancers are not needed for high availability
Backups are optional in fault-tolerant systems

questions.question: How do you optimize AWS costs for a high-traffic web application?
questions.answer:
Cost optimization strategies:

1. Use AWS Cost Explorer and Budgets to monitor spending
2. Use Reserved Instances for predictable workloads
3. Use Spot Instances for flexible, fault-tolerant workloads
4. Implement Auto Scaling based on demand
5. Optimize storage with S3 lifecycle policies
6. Use S3 Intelligent-Tiering for unpredictable access
7. Implement caching with CloudFront and ElastiCache
8. Choose right-sized instances based on actual usage
wrongAnswers: On-demand instances are the cheapest option for all workloads
Auto Scaling should only scale up, never down
Caching does not help reduce costs

questions.question: What are the key differences between batch and real-time data processing?
questions.answer:
Batch vs Real-time processing:

Batch Processing:
- Collects data over time, processes in large chunks
- Good for historical, less frequent data analysis
- Lower processing costs
- Complex modeling and forecasting

Real-time (Streaming):
- Processes data continuously as it arrives
- Immediate insights and analysis
- Good for fraud detection, social media monitoring
- Low latency requirements like trading systems

Choose batch for historical analysis, real-time for immediate insights.
wrongAnswers: Batch processing provides lower latency than real-time
Real-time processing is always more cost-effective
Both methods process data the same way

questions.question: When would you choose Amazon Kinesis over AWS Lambda for data processing?
questions.answer:
Choose Kinesis over Lambda when:

- High data volume: Kinesis handles MB/s vs Lambda's 6MB/invocation
- Streaming processing: Continuous real-time processing vs batch invocations
- Replay capability: Kinesis retains data for reprocessing
- Ordering: Kinesis shards ensure ordered processing
- Scaling: Kinesis shards scale for high throughput
- Integration: Better with Firehose, Redshift, EMR for analytics
wrongAnswers: Lambda is always better for high-volume streaming data
Kinesis cannot retain data for replay
Lambda functions can process millions of events per second

questions.question: How do you handle schema-on-read vs schema-on-write in AWS data lakes?
questions.answer:
Schema handling in data lakes:

Schema-on-Read (Data Lakes):
- Raw, semi-structured data stored in S3
- Schema applied only during query time
- Tools: Athena, Redshift Spectrum
- Flexibility for diverse data sources

Schema-on-Write (Traditional):
- Structure enforced upfront
- Preferred for transactional data
- Tools: RDS, Redshift
- Strict data validation required

Data lakes offer flexibility; traditional DBs offer structure.
wrongAnswers: Schema-on-read enforces structure during data ingestion
Schema-on-write only works with data lakes
Data lakes cannot handle unstructured data

questions.question: How do you automate schema evolution in an AWS data pipeline?
questions.answer:
Automating schema evolution:

1. Use AWS Glue's dynamic frame for schema inference
2. Leverage Glue Data Catalog to track schema changes
3. Implement schema validation with AWS Deequ
4. Add custom logic in ETL scripts to log mismatches
5. Use Lambda for schema change notifications
6. Set up CloudWatch events for schema drift alerts
7. Document schema versions for audit trail
wrongAnswers: Schema evolution requires rewriting all historical data
AWS Glue does not support schema inference
Schema changes are automatically blocked in data pipelines

questions.question: What is an Operational Data Store (ODS) and how does it complement a data warehouse?
questions.answer:
ODS (Operational Data Store):
- Database for real-time business operations
- Acts as interim platform between transactional systems and data warehouse
- Provides real-time data for operations monitoring
- Integrates live data from multiple sources
- Optimized for fast queries

Data Warehouse:
- High-quality data for BI and reporting
- Historical, integrated data
- Strategic reporting and analysis

ODS + Data Warehouse = comprehensive platform for operations and analytics
wrongAnswers: ODS replaces the need for data warehouses
ODS stores only historical, never current data
ODS cannot integrate data from multiple sources

questions.question: How would you set up a data lake on AWS?
questions.answer:
AWS Data Lake Setup:

1. Storage Layer: Amazon S3 for raw, structured, unstructured data
2. Data Cataloging: AWS Glue for metadata definitions
3. Transformation: AWS Glue ETL to prepare data for analysis
4. Security: IAM and Lake Formation for access control
5. Analytics: Athena for ad-hoc queries, Redshift Spectrum
6. Visualization: Amazon QuickSight for dashboards

Organize S3 with structured folder hierarchy by data type/source.
wrongAnswers: Data lakes only store structured data in databases
AWS Glue is not needed for data lakes
Data lake architecture requires physical servers

questions.question: What are the different S3 storage classes and when to use each?
questions.answer:
S3 Storage Classes:

| Class | Use Case | Access | Cost |
|-------|----------|--------|------|
| S3 Standard | Frequently accessed | High | Standard |
| Intelligent-Tiering | Unpredictable patterns | Auto | Cost-effective |
| Standard-IA | Infrequent, quick retrieval | Low | Lower + retrieval fee |
| One Zone-IA | Single AZ, infrequent | Low | Lower, less redundancy |
| Glacier | Long-term archival | Rare | Lowest |
| Glacier Deep Archive | Compliance, 12+ years | Very rare | Lowest ever |
wrongAnswers: S3 Standard is the cheapest storage class
S3 Glacier requires 24 hours to retrieve any data
Intelligent-Tiering has higher costs than S3 Standard

questions.question: How do you use AWS CodePipeline to automate a CI/CD pipeline?
questions.answer:
AWS CodePipeline CI/CD automation:

1. Create Pipeline: Specify source repository (GitHub, CodeCommit)
2. Build Stage: Connect CodeBuild to compile, run tests, create artifacts
3. Deploy Stages: Configure for each tier using CodeDeploy, Beanstalk, or ECS
4. Add Approval Gates: Manual approvals for critical environments
5. Monitor: Track pipeline performance, iterate based on feedback
wrongAnswers: CodePipeline cannot integrate with GitHub
CodePipeline requires manual deployment steps
CodePipeline only works with AWS-native services

questions.question: What are blue/green deployment strategies on AWS?
questions.answer:
Blue/Green Deployments:

Concept: Run two identical environments - blue (current) and green (new)

AWS Implementation:
1. Deploy green environment with new version
2. Test green environment thoroughly
3. Shift traffic using Route 53 or ALB
4. Monitor for issues
5. If problems: instant rollback to blue
6. Keep blue running until green is stable

Services: Elastic Beanstalk, CodeDeploy, ECS

Benefits: Zero downtime, instant rollback, reduced risk
wrongAnswers: Blue-green deployment requires downtime during switchover
Blue-green is only for containerized applications
Route 53 cannot route traffic between environments

questions.question: How do you manage and secure secrets for CI/CD pipeline in AWS?
questions.answer:
Secrets Management in CI/CD:

1. AWS Secrets Manager:
   - Store API keys, DB passwords, certificates
   - Automatic rotation with Lambda
   - Integration with CodePipeline/CodeBuild

2. Systems Manager Parameter Store:
   - SecureString for encrypted parameters
   - Hierarchical structure
   - Free tier available

3. IAM Controls:
   - Granular access policies
   - Role-based access for pipeline
   - No hardcoded secrets in code
wrongAnswers: Secrets should be stored directly in source code
Parameter Store cannot store encrypted values
IAM roles are not needed for CI/CD pipeline access

questions.question: How do you use AWS Systems Manager in a production environment?
questions.answer:
AWS Systems Manager in Production:

Use Cases:
1. Patch Management: Auto-apply OS/app patches across instances
2. Run Command: Execute scripts on multiple instances
3. Inventory: Collect software inventory from instances
4. Parameter Store: Store configs and secrets securely
5. Session Manager: Secure shell access without SSH
6. Automation: Trigger runbooks for operational tasks
7. State Manager: Enforce consistent configurations
wrongAnswers: Systems Manager requires SSH access to all instances
Systems Manager cannot patch operating systems
Systems Manager is only for monitoring, not automation

questions.question: What is the relationship between AWS Glue and AWS Lake Formation?
questions.answer:
AWS Glue and Lake Formation relationship:

AWS Glue provides:
- ETL capabilities for data transformation
- Control console and Data Catalog
- Serverless architecture for processing

AWS Lake Formation builds on Glue:
- Adds features for building data lakes
- Security and access management
- Data governance and auditing

In essence: Lake Formation = Glue + Data Lake Management

Glue handles ETL; Lake Formation handles security and governance.
wrongAnswers: Lake Formation replaces AWS Glue entirely
Glue cannot perform ETL operations
Lake Formation is only for small data sets under 1TB

questions.question: What factors should be considered in designing a deployment solution on AWS?
questions.answer:
AWS Deployment Design Factors:

1. Provisioning: Set up EC2, VPC, S3, RDS, CloudFront infrastructure
2. Configuring: Adjust for environment, security, availability, performance
3. Deploying: Efficiently roll out updates with smooth transitions
4. Scaling: Dynamically modify resources based on demand
5. Monitoring: Track usage, health, logs continuously
6. Security: Implement IAM, encryption, network controls
7. Cost: Right-size resources, use appropriate pricing models
wrongAnswers: Security is optional in initial deployment design
Cost considerations should be addressed after deployment
Monitoring is only needed after production launch

questions.question: How would you implement a microservices architecture on AWS?
questions.answer:
Microservices Architecture on AWS:

1. Adopt Agile Development for rapid iteration
2. Use API-First Design for clear service contracts
3. Implement CI/CD with CodePipeline/CodeBuild
4. Containerize with ECS/EKS or use Lambda
5. Apply Twelve-Factor App principles
6. Choose API-driven or event-driven patterns
7. Implement service discovery
8. Add distributed monitoring with CloudWatch/X-Ray
9. Design for fault tolerance and resilience
10. Use Well-Architected Framework for review
wrongAnswers: Microservices must all use the same programming language
Microservices should share a single database
CI/CD is not needed for microservices

questions.question: What is the difference between Amazon Redshift, RDS, and S3? When to use each?
questions.answer:
S3 vs RDS vs Redshift:

Amazon S3:
- Object storage for raw, unstructured data
- Log files, CSVs, images, backups
- Infinite scalability, pay per usage

Amazon RDS:
- Managed relational databases (PostgreSQL, MySQL, etc.)
- ACID-compliant transactions
- Indexing, constraints, complex queries
- Transactional applications

Amazon Redshift:
- Cloud data warehouse for analytics
- Complex queries over large datasets
- Columnar storage, massive parallelism
- BI and business intelligence
wrongAnswers: S3 is a database that supports SQL queries natively
RDS is optimized for analytical queries over petabytes
Redshift replaces the need for any database

questions.question: What considerations for migrating an on-premises application to AWS?
questions.answer:
AWS Migration Considerations:

1. Assessment: Catalog components, identify dependencies
2. Strategy: Choose migration techniques (lift-and-shift, replatform, refactor)
3. Network: Plan connection (Direct Connect or VPN)
4. Security: Implement IAM, encryption, compliance
5. Data: Use DMS for database migration
6. Testing: Validate functionality, performance
7. Cutover: Plan minimal downtime migration
8. Post-migration: Monitor, optimize, train team

Use AWS Migration Hub for tracking.
wrongAnswers: All applications must be migrated at once
Network connectivity planning is not necessary
AWS migration tools only work with AWS-native databases

questions.question: Design a scalable web application with DDoS protection on AWS.
questions.answer:
Scalable Web App with DDoS Protection:

Architecture:
1. CloudFront: Edge caching, DDoS protection layer 1-3
2. Route 53: DNS management with health checks
3. WAF: Filter malicious requests, rate limiting
4. Shield: Additional DDoS protection for layer 3-7
5. ALB: Distribute traffic across instances
6. ASG: Auto-scale EC2 across multiple AZs
7. RDS (Multi-AZ): Fault-tolerant database
8. ElastiCache: Session and data caching
9. CloudWatch: Monitoring and alerting
wrongAnswers: A single EC2 instance is sufficient for DDoS protection
WAF is not needed if using CloudFront
Auto Scaling does not help with DDoS attacks

questions.question: Design a disaster recovery solution with RPO of 5 minutes and RTO of 1 hour.
questions.answer:
DR Solution (RPO: 5min, RTO: 1hr):

Services Used:
1. AWS Backup: Regular backups with 5-minute RPO
2. CloudFormation: Define DR infrastructure as code
3. S3 Cross-Region Replication: Replicate backups across regions
4. CloudWatch Alarms: Monitor and trigger failover
5. Route 53: DNS failover to DR region

Strategy:
- Warm standby in secondary region
- Automated failover based on health checks
- Regular DR drills and testing
wrongAnswers: A secondary region is not needed for disaster recovery
AWS Backup cannot replicate data between regions
RTO of 1 hour means data loss of up to 1 hour

questions.question: What is AWS Glue and what are its main components?
questions.answer:
AWS Glue - Serverless ETL service:

Components:
1. Data Catalog: Centralized metadata repository
2. Crawlers: Auto-discover schemas from data sources
3. ETL Jobs: Transform and load data
4. Triggers: Schedule or event-based execution
5. Dev Endpoints: Interactive development
6. Dynamic Frames: Schema evolution handling

Integrations: S3, RDS, Redshift, DynamoDB

Use Cases: Data migration, data lake preparation, analytics pipeline
wrongAnswers: Glue crawlers can only discover JSON data formats
Glue is a compute service, not an ETL service
Glue requires managing underlying servers

questions.question: What is the AWS Well-Architected Framework and its five pillars?
questions.answer:
AWS Well-Architected Framework - Five Pillars:

1. Operational Excellence: Run/monitor systems, improve processes
2. Security: Protect data, systems through risk management
3. Reliability: Recover from failures, maintain availability
4. Performance Efficiency: Optimize resources, adapt to changes
5. Cost Optimization: Minimize expenses, maximize value

Each pillar provides best practices and design principles for building secure, high-performing, resilient, and efficient infrastructure.
wrongAnswers: Cost optimization is the most important pillar
Security should be addressed after other pillars
Reliability pillar only covers compute resources

questions.question: What is the difference between IAM Users, Groups, Roles, and Policies?
questions.answer:
IAM Components:

Users:
- Individual identities for people or applications
- Long-term credentials (access keys, passwords)
- Direct access to AWS services

Groups:
- Collection of users with shared permissions
- Easier permission management
- Apply policies to multiple users at once

Roles:
- Temporary permissions for trusted entities
- No permanent credentials
- Used by EC2, Lambda, cross-account access

Policies:
- JSON documents defining permissions
- Attached to users, groups, or roles
- Can be AWS-managed or customer-managed
wrongAnswers: IAM users and roles are identical concepts
Policies are only attached to individual users
Groups cannot have multiple policies attached

questions.question: What is Amazon EFS and when would you use it?
questions.answer:
Amazon EFS - Elastic File System:

What it is:
- Managed NFS (Network File System) file storage
- Scalable, pay-per-use
- Multi-AZ for high availability

Use Cases:
- Shared storage for multiple EC2 instances
- CMS and content management systems
- Code repositories and build systems
- Big data and analytics workloads
- Container storage (ECS, EKS)

vs EBS: EFS scales automatically, supports multiple connections
wrongAnswers: EFS can only be attached to a single EC2 instance
EFS is block storage like EBS
EFS does not support multi-AZ deployment

questions.question: What is AWS Snowball and what are its use cases?
questions.answer:
AWS Snowball - Physical data transfer:

Types:
- Snowball: 80TB storage, standard transfer
- Snowball Edge: 100TB with compute (Lambda, S3)
- Snowmobile: Container, up to 100PB

Use Cases:
- Large-scale data migration (TB/PB scale)
- Limited or no internet connectivity
- Disaster recovery data transfer
- Cloud migration for large enterprises

Benefits: Fast (Gbps speeds), secure (encrypted), cost-effective
wrongAnswers: Snowball is for computing workloads at the edge
Snowball requires internet connectivity for data transfer
Snowball Edge has less storage capacity than standard Snowball

questions.question: What is AWS Simple Notification Service (SNS)?
questions.answer:
Amazon SNS - Pub/Sub messaging and mobile notifications:

Features:
- Pub/Sub: One message to many subscribers
- Push model: Pushes to multiple endpoints
- Topics: Organize subscribers by category

Supported Subscribers:
- Lambda, SQS, HTTP/S
- Email, SMS, Mobile push
- Kinesis Data Firehose

Use Cases:
- Application alerts and notifications
- Event-driven architectures
- Fan-out patterns
- Mobile push notifications
wrongAnswers: SNS requires consumers to poll for messages
SNS topics can only have one subscriber
SNS cannot send mobile push notifications

questions.question: What is AWS Simple Queue Service (SQS) and the difference between Standard and FIFO queues?
questions.answer:
Amazon SQS - Managed message queuing:

Standard Queue:
- At-least-once delivery
- Best-effort ordering
- Unlimited throughput
- Millions of messages per second

FIFO Queue:
- Exactly-once processing
- Guaranteed ordering (first-in-first-out)
- Limited to 300 TPS
- No duplicates

Shared Features:
- Message retention (1 min to 14 days)
- Dead-letter queues
- Visibility timeout
wrongAnswers: FIFO queues guarantee at-least-once delivery
Standard queues process messages in strict order
SQS messages are automatically deleted after 24 hours

questions.question: What is AWS Elastic Beanstalk and when should you use it?
questions.answer:
AWS Elastic Beanstalk - PaaS for deploying apps:

What it is:
- Managed platform for deploying apps
- Supports: Node.js, Python, Java, .NET, Go, Docker
- Handles provisioning, scaling, monitoring

Use Cases:
- Quick deployment without infrastructure knowledge
- Simple web applications
- Microservices architecture
- API backends

Benefits:
- No additional cost (pay for underlying resources)
- Auto-scaling and load balancing
- Multiple environment support
wrongAnswers: Elastic Beanstalk requires managing all underlying infrastructure
Beanstalk only supports Java applications
Beanstalk cannot auto-scale applications

questions.question: What is AWS Global Accelerator and how does it improve performance?
questions.answer:
AWS Global Accelerator:

What it is:
- Networking service that improves availability and performance
- Routes traffic through AWS edge locations
- Uses anycast IP addresses

How it works:
1. Traffic enters nearest edge location
2. Routes over AWS network (not public internet)
3. Reaches your application in one or more regions

Benefits:
- 60% performance improvement for global apps
- Instant failover to healthy endpoints
- DDoS protection with Shield Standard
- Consistent performance regardless of location
wrongAnswers: Global Accelerator routes traffic over the public internet
Global Accelerator is only for Lambda functions
Global Accelerator cannot improve latency to EC2 instances

questions.question: What is AWS WAF and what protections does it offer?
questions.answer:
AWS WAF - Web Application Firewall:

Protections:
- SQL injection and XSS filtering
- IP blocking/allowing
- Rate limiting (prevent DDoS)
- Geo-blocking
- Bot control
- Custom rules with regex

Integration Points:
- CloudFront
- Application Load Balancer
- API Gateway
- AppSync

Managed Rules:
- AWS managed rule groups
- Third-party vendor rules (Fortinet, CrowdStrike)
wrongAnswers: AWS WAF only protects against SQL injection attacks
WAF can only be used with EC2 instances
AWS WAF does not support rate limiting
